perturbation.original_text: As used herein, "Confi dential Information" shall mean any and all oral and written proprietary informatio n provided by one party (the "Provider") to the other (the "Recipient"), includi ng but not limited to financial documents, customer lists, development plans, busin ess plans, trade secrets, and intellectual property.
perturbation.changed_text: As used herein, "Confidential Information" shall mean any and all information, regardless of whether it is proprietary, provided by one party (the "Provider") to the other (the "Recipient"), including but not limited to financial documents, customer lists, development plans, business plans, ideas, and intellectual property.
perturbation.explanation: The original text limits Confidential Information to 'proprietary information.' The modified text broadens the definition to include 'all information, regardless of whether it is proprietary.' This contradicts the accepted understanding and legal definitions of trade secrets, which require a proprietary nature.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA and corresponding state laws define trade secrets as information that derives independent economic value from not being generally known and is subject to reasonable efforts to maintain its secrecy. By including 'all information, regardless of whether it is proprietary,' the modified text attempts to protect information that does not meet the legal definition of a trade secret, potentially hindering enforcement.

perturbation.justification: The modified text broadens the definition of 'Confidential Information' to include 'all information, regardless of whether it is proprietary,' which contradicts the established legal understanding of trade secrets. The Law Explanation highlights that trade secrets derive economic value from not being generally known and are subject to reasonable secrecy efforts. By including non-proprietary information, the modified text seeks to protect information that does not meet the legal criteria for a trade secret, potentially hindering enforcement, which is also a violation of the defined 'Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction'. Though the snippets don't directly support this claim, based on all other information, a contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: Confidential Inform ation shall not be reproduced in any form except as required to accomplish the inten t of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information shall be freely reproduced in any form, regardless of whether it is required to accomplish the intent of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original text restricts reproduction to instances required to accomplish the agreement's intent. The modified text allows unrestricted reproduction. This undermines the core purpose of the NDA, which is to protect confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Contract Law - Consideration and Mutual Assent
perturbation.law_explanation: General contract law requires consideration and mutual assent. By allowing free reproduction of confidential information, the agreement essentially nullifies the 'confidentiality' aspect, removing the value one party receives (protection of their information), thus impacting consideration and the very intent of the agreement. If there's no protection, it can be argued there is no consideration, and the agreement is unenforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: consideration | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you consideration Consideration is a promise , performance, or forbearance bargained by a promisor in exchange for their promise. Consideration is the main element of a contract . Without consideration by both parties, a contract cannot be enforceable. For instance, if a person used the money to purchase an apple, the apple is the merchant’s consideration, and the money is the person’s consideration. Types: Consideration could be a promise, performance, forbearance, or property with legal value, but the economic benefit is not required. A gift or gratuitous promise cannot be a consideration for they have no bargaining. The past performance also cannot be a consideration as there is no exchange. Substitute for consideration: A contract without consideration could be enforceable if it has a substitute. Substitutes are promissory estoppel or detrimental reliance under Restatement (Second) of Contracts , or good faith modification under UCC . Promissory estoppel /detrimental reliance: A contract without consideration is enforceable if the nonperformance of the promisor will cause injustice. Elements of promissory estoppel are (i) the promise has reasonable, foreseeable, and detrimental reliance on the promisor, and (ii) the enforcement of the promise is necessary to avoid injustice. Good faith modification: A modified contract is a kind of new agreement, which changes parties’ obligations and then requires new consideration. But contract modification made in good faith under UCC is enforceable even without consideration. [Last reviewed in July of 2022 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE commercial activities business law contracts wex definitions business sectors commercial transactions legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the intent of the original agreement regarding confidential information. The original text restricts reproduction to instances required to accomplish the agreement's intent, while the modified text allows unrestricted reproduction, which negates the purpose of protecting confidential information. This lack of protection impacts consideration, as the protection of information is a key value one party receives, which is the main element of a contract. As the scraped snippet 1 states, 'Without consideration by both parties, a contract cannot be enforceable.'

perturbation.original_text: Upon termination or expir ation of the Agreement, or upon written request by either party, each party shall p romptly return to the other all documents and other tangible materials representing the other's Confidential Information and all copies thereof, or destroy all Confidential Information and copies thereof and certify its destruction to the Provider .
perturbation.changed_text: Upon termination or expiration of the Agreement, each party may, at their sole discretion, retain or return all documents and other tangible materials representing the other's Confidential Information and all copies thereof. Certification of destruction is optional.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates the return or destruction of confidential information upon termination. The modified text makes this discretionary, contradicting the fundamental purpose of an NDA, which is to prevent the continued use of confidential information after the agreement ends.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification allows for the continued possession of confidential information after the NDA terminates, potentially leading to misappropriation under trade secret laws. 18 U.S. Code § 1836 addresses the protection of trade secrets; retaining them after the agreement is over removes the protection that agreement intended to provide, therefore allowing the other party to misappropriate those trade secrets.

Civil proceedings (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (b) Private Civil Actions.- (1) In general .-An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.- (A) In
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the purpose of an NDA by allowing a party to retain confidential information after the agreement terminates. The original text requires return or destruction of confidential information, aligning with the protection of trade secrets. The modified text makes this action discretionary, potentially leading to misappropriation. While Scraped Law Snippet 1, 18 USC 1836, primarily discusses civil proceedings related to trade secret misappropriation, the Law Explanation posits that retaining trade secrets after the agreement undermines the protection that the agreement intended to provide, thereby facilitating misappropriation. By making the return or destruction of confidential information optional, the revised contract weakens the legal safeguards against potential trade secret violations that the original contract aimed to establish. This directly undermines the intent to protect confidential information, thus creating a significant legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement and all disputes risi ng hereunder shall be governed by the laws of the State of New York without regard to its conflict of laws provisions.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement and all disputes arising hereunder shall be governed by the laws of the State of North Korea without regard to its conflict of laws provisions.
perturbation.explanation: Specifying North Korean law as the governing law raises enforceability issues, as North Korean law is unlikely to be recognized or enforceable in US courts, especially regarding commercial disputes. It could also violate public policy.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Public Policy Doctrine in Contract Law
perturbation.law_explanation: The public policy doctrine allows courts to refuse to enforce contracts that violate fundamental principles of justice or morality. Choosing North Korean law, which is known for human rights violations and lack of due process, is likely to be considered against public policy and could render the entire agreement unenforceable in a US court. The Restatement addresses when a contract is unenforceable on grounds of public policy. A contract that violates the public policy of the United States is unenforceable.
perturbation.justification: The changed text specifies that the laws of North Korea govern the agreement. The Law Explanation states that using North Korean law could be against public policy, especially in US courts, given North Korea's human rights record and lack of due process. While Scraped Law Snippet 2 is unreadable, the Law Explanation strongly suggests that choosing North Korean law would likely render the agreement unenforceable in a US court due to public policy concerns. Therefore, the modified clause creates a legal contradiction by potentially violating fundamental principles of justice and morality as understood in the US, making the contract unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that one party breaches or threatens to breach this agreement the parties agree that monetary damages w ill be an inadequate remedy, and therefore, the other party will be entitled to an i mmediate permanent injunction against such breach and such other equitable relief as is n ecessary to enforce any and all of the provisions of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that one party breaches or threatens to breach this agreement, the parties agree that monetary damages will be an adequate remedy, and therefore, the other party will only be entitled to monetary damages. No injunctive relief will be available.
perturbation.explanation: The original text states that monetary damages are inadequate and allows for injunctive relief. The modified text reverses this, stating monetary damages are adequate and prohibiting injunctive relief. This significantly weakens the protection offered by the NDA, as injunctive relief is often crucial in preventing ongoing harm from the disclosure of confidential information. This limits the remedies available, even if damages are hard to calculate.
perturbation.law_citation: Federal Rules of Civil Procedure Rule 65
perturbation.law_explanation: Injunctive relief is a traditional equitable remedy available when monetary damages are insufficient to address the harm. By explicitly waiving the right to injunctive relief, the modified text may be considered unconscionable or against public policy, especially if the breach involves the disclosure of highly sensitive trade secrets where the harm is difficult to quantify. Rule 65 outlines the procedure of injunctive relief in federal courts, meaning that you are foregoing an important remedy under the law by agreeing to be limited to monetary damages.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 65. Injunctions and Restraining Orders | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 65. Injunctions and Restraining Orders (a) Preliminary Injunction. (1) Notice. The court may issue a preliminary injunction only on notice to the adverse party. (2) Consolidating the Hearing with the Trial on the Merits. Before or after beginning the hearing on a motion for a preliminary injunction, the court may advance the trial on the merits and consolidate it with the hearing. Even when consolidation is not ordered, evidence that is received on the motion and that would be admissible at trial becomes part of the trial record and need not be repeated at trial. But the court must preserve any party's right to a jury trial. (b) Temporary Restraining Order. (1) Issuing Without Notice. The court may issue a temporary restraining order without written or oral notice to the adverse party or its attorney only if: (A) specific facts in an affidavit or a verified complaint clearly show that immediate and irreparable injury, loss, or damage will result to the movant before the adverse party can be heard in opposition; and (B) the movant's attorney certifies in writing any efforts made to give notice and the reasons why it should not be required. (2) Contents; Expiration. Every temporary restraining order issued without notice must state the date and hour it was issued; describe the injury and state why it is irreparable; state why the order was issued without notice; and be promptly filed in the clerk's office and entered in the record. The order expires at the time after entry—not to exceed 14 days—that the court sets, unless before that time the court, for good cause, extends it for a like period or the adverse party consents to a longer extension. The reasons for an extension must be entered in the record. (3) Expediting the Preliminary-Injunction Hearing. If the order is issued without notice, the moti

Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The original contract allows for injunctive relief in case of a breach, stating that monetary damages would be inadequate. The modified text explicitly states that monetary damages are an adequate remedy and prohibits injunctive relief. This directly contradicts the availability of remedies typically afforded in contract law, especially considering the Law Explanation's mention of injunctive relief being a traditional equitable remedy when monetary damages are insufficient. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing Rule 65 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure, concerns injunctions and restraining orders, illustrating the legal framework for such remedies in federal courts. By waiving injunctive relief, the modified text potentially weakens the protection offered, particularly when damages are hard to calculate, creating a significant legal contradiction.
